title: How to run an app takeover audit | Bc. Ondrej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: How to run an app takeover audit | Bc. Ondrej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: A practical guide to app takeover audits: what to verify before inheriting a system, how to frame risks, and how to shape the first credible next step.

page.title: How to run an app takeover audit | Bc. Ondrej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: How to run an app takeover audit before inheriting an existing system
page.description: A practical guide to app takeover audits: what to verify before inheriting a system, how to frame risks, and how to shape the first credible next step.

hero.title: Before taking over an application, you need more than repository access
hero.subtitle: A strong takeover audit separates technical risk, operational dependency, and first-phase priorities from the illusion that handover alone will solve the situation.

intro: Taking over an existing application is not only about code. You need to understand how the system runs, where the operational dependencies sit, how release works, and what a bad first change could break.
A takeover audit is therefore not about creating a thick document. It is about getting a fast, reliable picture of risks, weak spots, and the most credible next step.

sections.title: What the audit has to clarify
body: The audit should map not only the application itself, but also environment, release path, access, data, integrations, and the knowledge concentration around the system.
bullets: access to repositories, hosting, and third parties
release process, monitoring, and rollback options
state of documentation, knowledge, and ownership

sections.title: How to separate facts from assumptions
body: Inherited systems often come with many claims based on impressions. A practical audit needs to distinguish what is confirmed, what is still a hypothesis, and what needs further verification.

sections.title: What should follow the audit
body: The point is not the audit itself. The output should be a prioritised next step: what to stabilise first, what to address for delivery speed, and what can wait.

sections.title: What to avoid
body: The weakest option is to skip the audit and immediately promise fixes, a rewrite, or a fast takeover without understanding the operational and release risk.

faq.question: Do we still need an audit if we already have code access?
faq.answer: Yes. Code access alone says very little about release risk, operational dependency, or how people actually use the system.

faq.question: How detailed should the audit be?
faq.answer: Detailed enough to support the first reliable decision about takeover, stabilisation, and next priorities. Not necessarily detailed enough to document every corner of the product.

faq.question: Can the audit conclude that incremental improvement is better than a rewrite?
faq.answer: Yes. That is often the most valuable conclusion because it separates the actual problem from frustration with inherited code.
